hero.tagline: Iowa's small claims court offers a streamlined process for resolving disputes up to $6,500, with simplified procedures and quick resolutions.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $6,500 cap, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the small claims limit. You must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Iowa allows you to file claims up to $6,500 for disputes involving contracts and money owed. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 10-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, and they failed to repay it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $3,000 to a colleague with the understanding it would be repaid in six months, but they haven't paid you back.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide documentation of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $5,000 over a year but stopped making payments after two months.
claims.notes: 10-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $4,500 to a client who has not paid the invoice issued three months ago.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Submit the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their account with your business and has ignored payment requests.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of payment history.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $1,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property without settling the debt.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods worth $800 with a check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from dishonor. Provide the bounced check and any bank notices.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount plus service charge up to $40 plus civil penalty up to $100 or 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a loan for $5,000, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible for the debt.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide the loan agreement and records of payments made.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Iowa's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $6,500, including issues with products and services.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from delivery. Bring the purchase receipt and any warranty information.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $500 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks satisfactorily.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and evidence of poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and the car remains inoperable.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Present repair invoices and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Incomplete or poor-quality contractor work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $6,000 to renovate your bathroom, but the work was left unfinished and subpar.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from contract date. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You hired a DJ for $1,000 for your wedding, but they never showed up, and you had to find a replacement last minute.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from service date. Present the service agreement and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your account for three months.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $5,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the bill of sale and any repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $300 with features it doesn't have, leading you to make the purchase under false pretenses.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from discovery. Provide the advertisement and proof of purchase.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Iowa's small claims court addresses property damage claims up to $6,500, including vehicle collisions and damage to personal property.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,000 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from accident date. Provide police reports and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $1,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Present photos of the damage and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left significant damage to the apartment, totaling $4,000 in repair costs.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from lease end. Provide the lease agreement and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed landscaping worth $800.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Present photos of the damage and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your bicycle with a friend for safekeeping, and it was returned with $600 in damages.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from damage date. Provide evidence of the agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Iowa's small claims court handles monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $6,500, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection reports.

claims.name: Landlord failed to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Request the itemized list in writing before filing.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to make repairs.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from issue. Document all communication and conditions with photos.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's personal property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $2,000 without legal process.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from incident. Provide proof of ownership and any communication.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from eviction. Document the eviction process and any lack of notice.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after you moved out early with agreement.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and any written agreements about early termination.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months of rent totaling $2,400 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from missed payment. Provide the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent without proper procedure
claims.example: Your tenant deducted $500 from rent for repairs they made without notifying you or providing receipts.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from deduction. Provide the lease agreement and records of rent payments.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Iowa's small claims court addresses employment-related disputes up to $6,500, including unpaid wages and final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but has not issued payment.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from wage due date. Provide timesheets and any employment agreements.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to pay your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from termination. Document your resignation and any communication regarding the final paycheck.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions that your employer has not paid.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from commission due date. Provide sales records and commission agreements.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but were only paid your regular hourly rate.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from overtime worked. Provide timesheets and pay stubs.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Iowa's small claims court handles personal claims up to $6,500, including minor injuries and property disputes.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in medical bills totaling $2,000.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend your camera worth $1,200, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your lawnmower worth $800 without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from theft date. Provide proof of ownership and any communication.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Iowa's small claims court addresses vehicle-related disputes up to $6,500, including mechanic disputes and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $1,500 for repairs you didn't authorize.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices and any communication.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you had to pay $300 to retrieve it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from towing date. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot determine ownership of real property.
Eviction cases are handled under a different procedure.
Claims involving libel or slander are not suitable for small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limit.
Claims involving federal law are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Class action lawsuits are not permitted in small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring specific actions.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and wills are handled in specialized courts.
Such claims are outside the jurisdiction of state courts.
Claims for workplace injuries are handled by the workers' compensation system.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration agreements are not suitable for small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount plus service charge up to $40 plus civil penalty up to $100 or 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in certain cases as provided by statute.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the District Court

filing_options: Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing is available statewide through the Iowa Judicial Branch's eFile system.

forms.name: Application to Proceed In Forma Pauperis

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can facilitate settlement and demonstrate good faith.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before pursuing statutory penalties for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: A written demand for unpaid wages is recommended before filing a claim.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide a written itemization of damages within 30 days of lease termination.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Certain consumer protection claims may require prior notice to the defendant.

forms.name: Original Notice and Petition for a Money Judgment
forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for a money judgment.

forms.name: Original Notice and Petition for Forcible Entry and Detainer
forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for eviction proceedings.

forms.name: Original Notice and Petition for Detinue
forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for the recovery of specific personal property.

forms.name: Original Notice and Petition for Recovery of Property
forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for the recovery of property.

forms.name: Original Notice and Petition for Money Judgment and Recovery of Property
forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for both a money judgment and recovery of property.

forms.name: Original Notice and Petition for Money Judgment and Replevin
forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for both a money judgment and return of personal property.

forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for a money judgment, eviction, and return of personal property.

forms.description: Initiates a small claims action for a money judgment, eviction, and recovery of specific personal property.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Clerk sends the papers via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents delivers the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Iowa rules and the rules of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must appear at the hearing or risk a default judgment; a written answer is not required but may be filed.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal within 20 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to district court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to do so.
hearing.format: Bench trial, typically 30-60 minutes per case.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter through the court clerk at least 10 days before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 2 weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on the day of the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in district court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 20 days.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at the statutory rate.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File an application for garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.

forms.name: Application for Garnishment

methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds to satisfy the judgment.

forms.name: Writ of Execution

methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's property, which must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.

forms.name: Notice of Judgment Lien

methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, the sheriff seizes non-exempt property, sells it at auction, and applies proceeds to the judgment.

forms.name: Writ of Execution

methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a petition for renewal before the judgment expires; no fee required.
exempt_assets: Social Security and federal benefits — fully exempt
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are filed.

businesses.self_representation_notes: A business may be represented by an owner or officer without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Certificate of Assumed Name.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may represent themselves but cannot send an employee in their place.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts in court.

county_variations.detail: Polk County offers online filing through the Iowa Judicial Branch's eFile system.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue 'John's Garage' but the legal entity is 'JG Auto Repair LLC', the judgment may be unenforceable against the LLC's assets.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Search the Secretary of State business records before filing and use the exact registered name; for sole proprietors use the individual's full legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing one day after the deadline gets the case dismissed with prejudice.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the SOL for your specific claim type before filing; for fraud, the discovery rule applies but be prepared to prove when you reasonably could have discovered it.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant isn't properly served, the case can be delayed or dismissed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Iowa's service rules carefully; consider using the sheriff for service to ensure compliance.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: The case may be dismissed or transferred, causing delays and additional costs.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: File in the county where the defendant resides or where the cause of action arose.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a case doesn't guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to pursue collection methods like garnishment or liens if the defendant doesn't pay voluntarily.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking exemptions in collection
pitfalls.what_happens: Certain debtor assets are protected from collection, limiting recovery options.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Understand Iowa's exemption laws to assess the feasibility of collection efforts.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims jurisdictional limit
recent_changes.description: The monetary limit for small claims cases increased from $5,000 to $6,500, allowing more cases to be heard in small claims court.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves, though you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases are typically resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing, depending on the court's schedule and the complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: As of July 1, 2023, the maximum amount you can sue for in Iowa small claims court is $6,500, excluding interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor, provided you present sufficient evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims decision to the district court within 20 days of the judgment. The appeal will be a new trial (trial de novo) in district court.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: In Iowa, a judgment is valid for 20 years from the date it is entered. You may need to take steps to renew the judgment before it expires to maintain its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Iowa allows e-filing for small claims cases through the Iowa Judicial Branch's eFile system.
The small claims jurisdictional limit increased to $6,500 on July 1, 2023.
Small claims court procedures are designed to be informal and accessible to self-represented litigants.
Iowa law requires landlords to return security deposits or provide an itemized list of deductions within 30 days of lease termination.
Statutory penalties may apply for writing bad checks, including civil penalties up to $100 or double the amount of the check, whichever is greater.

interesting_findings: Iowa's small claims court allows for the recovery of attorney fees in certain cases as provided by statute.
The court may order mediation in small claims cases to encourage settlement before proceeding to a hearing.
Iowa's small claims procedures are governed by Iowa Code Chapter 631, which outlines the jurisdiction and process for these cases.
